segments.title: Peking Plan sends Polish destroyers to Britain
segments.summary: Poland sends destroyers through the Danish straits before the invasion so they can remain useful to the Allied war effort.
segments.body: The Peking Plan treated escape as a way to preserve combat power, not as a surrender. Polish destroyers were meant to reach Britain, support a possible Romanian bridgehead through escorted supply convoys, and avoid being trapped in the Baltic. After receiving the signal on 29 August, the ships passed German forces without fighting and reached Leith on 1 September 1939.

evidence.note: The transcript explains why Polish surface ships were to get away for British and Romanian supply plans.
The passage follows the signal, transit through the Danish straits, and arrival at Leith.

segments.title: Wilk turns a difficult escape into mine warfare
segments.summary: The Polish submarine Wilk escapes the Baltic after laying mines that later force a costly German search effort.
segments.body: Polish submarines were initially assigned to defend the Danzig and Hel area, but Wilk faced depth charges, technical problems, and the loss of shore support. Its minefield later sank a German fishing vessel even after the submarine had reached Britain. The delayed result shows why mines can make an opponent expend effort hunting a submarine that is no longer nearby.

evidence.note: Wilk's defensive mission, mine-laying, damage, and eventual arrival in Britain are described.
The transcript explains the later mine strike and the German search it triggered.

segments.title: French ships in British ports and the Surcouf seizure
segments.summary: French warships reach Britain in 1940, but changing political orders turn their safety into a tense Allied command problem.
segments.body: Courbet and Paris arrived with naval personnel during the evacuation from France, yet the French government's changing position made the ships a source of British anxiety as well as potential Allied assets. Surcouf reached Plymouth while disabled by a refit, then became one of the rare violent seizures during Operation Catapult. The episode separates British respect for the French Navy from its loss of confidence in political assurances about the fleet's future use.

evidence.note: The discussion covers French battleships in Britain, Free French service, and the uncertainty preceding Mers-el-Kebir.
Surcouf's damaged departure from Brest, the Plymouth boarding, casualties, and later Free French use are covered.

segments.title: Orzeł escapes Tallinn without its charts
segments.summary: Orzeł slips away from Tallinn after its charts are seized, navigating the Baltic by lighthouse references before reaching Britain.
segments.body: German pressure led Estonian authorities to strip navigation aids and torpedoes from the Polish submarine before the neutral-port time limit had elapsed. The crew sabotaged the torpedo hoist, overpowered the guards, and escaped with only a list of lighthouses as a navigational aid. A damaged radio then left Orzeł unable to identify itself safely in the North Sea until it made contact with a Royal Navy destroyer.

evidence.note: The Tallinn internment, removal of charts and torpedoes, and the crew's planned breakout are described.
The passage follows lighthouse navigation, the radio problem, and Orzeł's final contact with Britain.

segments.title: A Belgian patrol boat carries gold to Britain
segments.summary: A small Belgian patrol boat brings national gold reserves to Britain, giving the government in exile financial independence.
segments.body: The modest A4 carried roughly 40 to 50 tons of Belgian gold from Ostend to Plymouth as Belgium collapsed in May 1940. The shipment gave the government in exile independent funds instead of making it wholly dependent on Britain. After Belgium's surrender, the crew took the vessel to Bilbao and spent the war interned in Spain rather than return the ship to occupied territory.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Belgium's gold reserves, the A4's crossing, and the funds available to the government in exile.
The crew's decision to take A4 to Spain and the vessel's later return are described.

segments.title: Greek fleet withdrawal preserves an exile navy
segments.summary: Greek plans move each viable vessel toward Alexandria in stages, allowing the Hellenic Navy to grow during the war.
segments.body: Greek planners expected little outside help and began moving ships toward Alexandria as soon as the war began. Their staged plan withdrew each asset once enemy forces reached defined points, while merchant shipping provided vital carrying capacity. The result was an exile navy that grew from a little over 6,000 people to about 12,000 by 1944 despite combat and air attack during the withdrawal.

evidence.note: The speaker describes Greek contingency planning, early movement to Alexandria, and the fleet's initial strength.
The completed withdrawal, merchant marine support, and wartime growth of the Hellenic Navy are discussed.

segments.title: Draug captures a German supply ship and reaches the Shetlands
segments.summary: The aging Norwegian destroyer Draug captures a German vessel carrying mines and supplies, then escapes with prisoners after air attack.
segments.body: Draug had only just returned from an arduous reactivation when the German invasion began, yet its captain acted on his own judgement after receiving warning that Norwegian command might be compromised. The destroyer seized a German merchant ship whose supposed coke cargo concealed supplies and about 2,000 mines for forces heading to Trondheim. Luftwaffe attacks sank the prize before it could reach Britain, but Draug brought its prisoners and crew safely to the Shetlands.

evidence.note: The transcript covers Draug's difficult reactivation, mobilisation, and decision to act during the invasion.
The capture, concealed military cargo, air attack, scuttling of the prize, and arrival in the Shetlands are described.

segments.title: Niels Juel attempts a Danish dash to Sweden
segments.summary: Danish plans call for ships to flee to Sweden or scuttle themselves; Niels Juel attempts the dash but is forced ashore under air attack.
segments.body: With Germany preparing to seize the Danish fleet in August 1943, Danish crews had standing orders to escape to Sweden if possible or deny their ships to the enemy. Niels Juel tried to slip away, initially withholding fire in hopes of avoiding escalation, but Luftwaffe attacks eventually destroyed its power and fire-control capacity. The ship was beached and scuttled, while the closing discussion argues that nations need practical extraction arrangements before a strategic collapse makes choices impossible.

evidence.note: The Danish contingency orders, Niels Juel's breakout attempt, air attack, and scuttling are covered.
The conclusion argues for national force-extraction plans and prearranged places to withdraw.
